metadata.title: Question-to-Theory Mapping Reference
metadata.purpose: Injected into the Stage 1 LLM prompt as an explicit theoretical grounding reference. Tells the LLM exactly which RIASEC types and SCCT constructs each question is designed to elicit, so signal extraction is guided by the researcher's framework rather than the LLM's implicit interpretation.
metadata.usage: Load this file in stage1_career_theory.py and embed the mappings inside the extraction prompt context block.
theoretical_sources: Holland, J. L. (1997). Making vocational choices: A theory of vocational personalities and work environments (3rd ed.).
Lent, R. W., Brown, S. D., & Hackett, G. (1994). Toward a unifying social cognitive theory of career and academic interest, choice, and performance.

riasec_definitions.description: Six Holland RIASEC personality-environment types. A student's dominant type indicates the kind of work environment and activities they are most naturally drawn to.

Realistic.enjoys: Working with tools, machines, nature, physical systems, or the body
Realistic.avoids: Abstract ideas, heavy social interaction, paperwork

Investigative.enjoys: Research, analysis, solving complex problems, understanding how things work
Investigative.avoids: Repetitive tasks, leading others, sales or persuasion
example_signals: I like figuring out why things happen

Artistic.enjoys: Creating, designing, performing, writing, expressing ideas
Artistic.avoids: Rigid rules, repetitive structured tasks, heavy data work
example_signals: I express myself through art or storytelling

Social.enjoys: Teaching, counseling, helping, working with people, community involvement
Social.avoids: Working alone, machines, physical or technical tasks
example_signals: I feel fulfilled when I support people

Enterprising.enjoys: Leading, selling, managing, influencing, starting things
Enterprising.avoids: Detailed analytical tasks, following rather than leading
example_signals: I want to run my own business

Conventional.enjoys: Managing data, records, finances, following procedures, structured environments
example_signals: I enjoy working with numbers and records

scct_constructs.description: Two SCCT constructs extracted from student responses. These are distinct from RIASEC and capture the student's belief in their own abilities and their vision of career outcomes.

self_efficacy.definition: The student's belief in their own capability to perform specific tasks or succeed in specific competency domains.
self_efficacy.how_to_detect: Look for statements of competence, academic strength, skill claims with evidence, or confidence in a subject area. High self-efficacy = strong, confident, evidence-backed claims. Low self-efficacy = hesitation, avoidance, or explicit admission of weakness.
example_signals: I am really good at Math
I always get high grades in Science
I can communicate well in English
People say I am a good listener
I have been the president of our club for 2 years

outcome_expectations.definition: The student's beliefs about the results or rewards that will follow from pursuing a specific career or academic path — what they expect their future to look like.
outcome_expectations.how_to_detect: Look for career aspirations, vision statements, reasons for wanting a particular job, or descriptions of what success means to them. These reveal what careers feel desirable or meaningful to the student.
example_signals: I want to be a teacher because I want to help students
I see myself running my own business
I want a career where I can help people with their mental health
I imagine myself working in a hospital
I want to make a difference in my community

question_theory_map.question_summary: What activities do you enjoy most in school, at home, or free time?
question_theory_map.extraction_guidance: This question surfaces natural activity preferences. Physical or hands-on activities signal Realistic. Creative or expressive activities signal Artistic. Research, reading, or puzzle-solving activities signal Investigative. Social activities here may also appear but are better confirmed in q3.
question_theory_map.watch_for_ambiguity: A student who says 'I enjoy helping my parents cook' could be Realistic (hands-on) or Social (family-oriented). Look for corroboration in other questions before assigning type.

question_theory_map.question_summary: How do you handle difficult problems? Give an example.
question_theory_map.extraction_guidance: Problem-solving style reveals type. Students who research independently, analyze data, or think deeply = Investigative. Students who try things physically or build solutions = Realistic. Students who ask others for help or discuss problems in groups = Social. The confidence with which they describe their approach is also a self-efficacy signal.
question_theory_map.watch_for_ambiguity: Some students may describe a hybrid (e.g., 'I research first, then ask my teacher'). Extract the dominant pattern.
question_theory_map.weight: MEDIUM — corroborative RIASEC + mild self-efficacy signal

question_theory_map.question_summary: Describe a time you helped someone, led a group, or contributed to community.
question_theory_map.extraction_guidance: Helping, teaching, counseling, and caregiving signals = Social. Leading, organizing, managing a group, motivating others signals = Enterprising. The emotional response ('it made me feel fulfilled / I want to do this again') is a direct SCCT outcome expectation signal — extract the career domain they associate with this fulfillment.
question_theory_map.watch_for_ambiguity: A student council president could be Social (if they talk about helping classmates) or Enterprising (if they talk about leading and making decisions). Focus on the motivation behind the action.
question_theory_map.weight: HIGH — primary Social/Enterprising discriminator + outcome expectation

question_theory_map.question_summary: What subjects are you good at and which do you struggle with?
question_theory_map.extraction_guidance: This is the PRIMARY self-efficacy question. Map subject strengths to competency domains: Math/Accounting → Quantitative/Analytical; Science/Biology → Natural Sciences; English/Filipino → Communication/Language; Social Studies/History → Social Science; Computer/TLE-ICT → Technology; Arts/Music → Creative Arts; PE/MAPEH → Physical/Health. High self-efficacy = strong grades + confidence. Low self-efficacy = explicit struggle or dislike.
question_theory_map.watch_for_ambiguity: Some students understate their strengths out of humility. Look for indirect confidence markers like 'I always pass' or 'my teachers say I'm good at it'.
question_theory_map.weight: VERY HIGH — primary SCCT self-efficacy source

question_theory_map.question_summary: If you could have any career regardless of money or opinions, what would it be and why?
question_theory_map.extraction_guidance: This is the PRIMARY career outcome expectation question. Extract the specific career named AND the reason given. The career name maps to a program. The reason reveals what the student values (helping people = Social/BSPSY/BSEd; making money/leading = Enterprising/BSBA; solving tech problems = Investigative+Realistic/BSIT; justice = Realistic+Social/BSCRIM). Both the career AND the motivation matter.
question_theory_map.watch_for_ambiguity: Some students may name a career outside the 9 programs (e.g., doctor, lawyer). Extract the underlying motivation and competency domain rather than the job title itself.
question_theory_map.weight: VERY HIGH — primary SCCT outcome expectation source

question_theory_map.question_summary: Which type of work feels most meaningful: physical/tools, research/analysis, creative, helping people, leading, or organizing?
question_theory_map.extraction_guidance: This is the DIRECT RIASEC elicitation question. The six choices map 1:1 to the six Holland types: (a)=Realistic, (b)=Investigative, (c)=Artistic, (d)=Social, (e)=Enterprising, (f)=Conventional. If a student selects multiple, treat them as a Holland code combination (e.g., SC, IE, EC). The explanation they give for their choice adds nuance — weight the explanation over the letter choice if they contradict each other.
question_theory_map.watch_for_ambiguity: Students may choose what sounds most prestigious (e.g., 'b' for Investigative) rather than what is truly them. Triangulate with q1 and q5 — if all three point to the same type, confidence is high.
question_theory_map.weight: HIGH — direct RIASEC discriminator; use to validate or challenge signals from q1 and q5

question_theory_map.question_summary: What are your top 3 personal strengths or skills with examples?
question_theory_map.extraction_guidance: This is the SECONDARY self-efficacy question, reinforcing q4 with behavioral evidence. Strengths with concrete examples = high self-efficacy in that domain. Vague strengths without evidence = moderate self-efficacy. Map stated strengths to competency domains: 'communication' → Communication; 'leadership' → Leadership/Management; 'empathy/listening' → Counseling/Social Work; 'math/numbers' → Quantitative Reasoning; 'computers' → Information Technology; 'organizing' → Administration/Management; 'research' → Research Methods.
question_theory_map.watch_for_ambiguity: Students often list generic strengths ('hardworking', 'responsible'). These are character traits, not competency domains — weight lower unless accompanied by domain-specific evidence.
question_theory_map.weight: HIGH — secondary SCCT self-efficacy source with behavioral evidence

question_theory_map.question_summary: Describe your life and work environment 10 years from now.
question_theory_map.extraction_guidance: This is the SECONDARY career outcome expectation question. Extract: (1) the setting they describe (school, hospital, office, outdoors, own business, government) — this maps to Holland environments; (2) what they are doing day-to-day — this maps to career outcomes; (3) who they work with — this reinforces Holland type (working with people=S/E, working with systems=C/R, working with ideas=I/A); (4) what impact they describe — this reveals value alignment and SCCT outcome expectations.
question_theory_map.watch_for_ambiguity: Some students describe idealistic futures detached from their stated strengths. Flag discrepancies between q4/q7 (self-efficacy) and q8 (outcome expectations) for the LLM to note in the extraction output.
question_theory_map.weight: HIGH — secondary SCCT outcome expectation; triangulates with q5

holland_code_assignment.rule: Assign a primary Holland type and optionally a secondary type (Holland code is typically 2-3 letters, e.g., 'SI', 'EC', 'IRC'). The primary type must be supported by at least 2 of the 8 questions. The secondary type must appear in at least 1 question.
holland_code_assignment.fallback: If signals are contradictory or weak, default to the type indicated by q6 (direct elicitation) as the tiebreaker.

self_efficacy_domains.rule: List only competency domains where the student shows HIGH confidence — meaning they explicitly claim strength, have evidence, or show academic performance. Domains where the student is weak or uncertain should NOT be included.

career_outcome_preferences.rule: List the top 2-3 career outcomes the student has expressed desire for, based on q5 and q8. Use general career categories (e.g., 'Teaching', 'Counseling', 'Law Enforcement') not specific job titles.

output_json_structure.description: The Stage 1 LLM must output exactly this JSON structure. No additional fields.

template.extraction_notes: Optional: flag any contradictions or ambiguities found across questions.
